title: Quiz: T Gates and Universal Quantum Computation
description: Test your understanding of universal gate sets, T gates, and gate synthesis

questions.question: Why is the T gate essential for universal quantum computation when combined with Clifford gates?

options.text: It is faster to implement than other gates
Clifford gates alone are efficiently classically simulable, and T provides the non-Clifford operation needed for universality
T gates create entanglement between qubits
T gates are the only gates that preserve the computational basis

questions.explanation: By the Gottesman-Knill theorem, Clifford circuits can be efficiently simulated classically. The T gate is non-Clifford and, when added to Clifford gates, enables universal quantum computation that cannot be efficiently simulated classically.

questions.question: How are T gates typically implemented in fault-tolerant quantum computing?

options.text: Direct physical implementation like Clifford gates
Via magic state injection using gate teleportation
By decomposing into many Hadamard gates
Using classical post-processing only

questions.explanation: T gates are implemented fault-tolerantly using magic state injection. A 'magic state' |T> = T|+> is prepared (often through distillation), and gate teleportation is used to apply the T gate to the target qubit.

questions.question: What does the Solovay-Kitaev theorem guarantee?

options.text: Any unitary can be exactly implemented with finitely many T gates
Any unitary can be approximated to precision epsilon using O(log^c(1/epsilon)) gates from a universal set
Clifford+T is the only universal gate set
T gates can be simulated classically

questions.explanation: The Solovay-Kitaev theorem states that any unitary can be approximated to precision epsilon using O(log^c(1/epsilon)) gates from a universal generating set, where c is approximately 4 for basic algorithms.

questions.question: What is T^4 equal to?

options.text: Identity (I)
The S gate
The Z gate
The X gate

questions.explanation: T = exp(i*pi/8)*diag(1, exp(i*pi/4)). So T^2 = S and T^4 = S^2 = Z. Also, T^8 = I (identity).

questions.question: The T-count of a circuit (number of T gates required) is an important metric for fault-tolerant quantum computing because T gates are 'expensive' to implement.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. T gates require magic state distillation, which consumes significant resources (many physical qubits and time). Reducing T-count is crucial for practical fault-tolerant quantum computation.

questions.question: What is a 'magic state' in the context of fault-tolerant quantum computing?

options.text: Any superposition state |+> or |->
A specially prepared resource state like |T> = T|+> used to implement T gates
A maximally entangled state between two qubits
A state that cannot be measured

questions.explanation: A magic state (like |T> = T|+>) is a specially prepared resource state that, combined with Clifford operations and measurement, allows implementation of non-Clifford gates like T through gate teleportation.

questions.question: What is the T-count of a standard Toffoli gate decomposition into Clifford+T?
questions.explanation: The standard decomposition of a Toffoli gate into Clifford+T requires 7 T gates. This can be reduced to 4 T gates using ancilla qubits and measurement-based techniques.

questions.question: Which of the following are members of the Clifford group? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Hadamard (H) gate
Phase (S) gate
T gate
CNOT gate

questions.explanation: The Clifford group is generated by H, S, and CNOT. The T gate is specifically NOT in the Clifford group - it is a level-3 gate in the Clifford hierarchy, and adding it to Clifford gates gives universality.
